new_findings.title: Commune-of-infection assignment protocol undocumented in main text
new_findings.evidence: cases reported before that date (n = 103) were originally registered under the SEREMI Biobío regional office and were assigned to their probable commune of infection—rather than residence—**following the case reconciliation protocol described in Supplementary Methods**
new_findings.mechanism: Main text relegates critical spatial exposure assignment (103/136 = 75.7% of cases) to supplement without summarizing criteria. If reconciliation protocol used residence as proxy when infection locale was ambiguous, or lacked standardized criteria, spatial misclassification could bias FSI lag estimates toward null or create spurious associations in high-mobility border communes
new_findings.fix_hint: Summarize reconciliation protocol criteria in main Methods (e.g., 'infection commune assigned via epidemiological interview when available [n=X], residence when interview incomplete [n=Y], or excluded when ambiguous [n=Z]') with sensitivity analysis excluding ambiguous assignments
new_findings.mcc_rationale: Main text references Supplementary Methods for protocol but no Q1 citation or explicit calculation visible in prior evidence. Grep returned empty snippets.

new_findings.title: ΔNBR2 < −0.15 wildfire veto threshold lacks empirical justification
new_findings.evidence: active-burn pixels vetoed (ΔNBR2 < −0.15) to prevent wildfire-scar contamination (Ecke et al. 2019)
new_findings.mechanism: Ecke 2019 studied Swedish rodent outbreaks post-fire, not Chilean Chusquea flowering. The −0.15 threshold is stated without validation against known Ñuble wildfire scars (2017 mega-fires affected quiloide zones). If threshold misclassifies post-fire regeneration as flowering signal, FSI becomes confounded by fire ecology; if too conservative, loses true flowering pixels
new_findings.fix_hint: Validate threshold against known 2017 Ñuble fire perimeters (CONAF registry). Show ΔNBR2 distribution for quiloide pixels inside vs outside fire scars. Perform sensitivity analysis with ±0.05 threshold variants
new_findings.mcc_rationale: Prior vault references GEE_FSI_Reconstruccion_S51.md and Ecke 2019 citation exists, but no empirical justification of -0.15 threshold calculation found in snippets.

new_findings.title: Peridomestic transmission assumption (87%) not verified in study sample
new_findings.evidence: 87% peridomestic transmission per Riquelme 2015 supports spatial accuracy; residual misclassification biases estimates toward null
new_findings.mechanism: Riquelme 2015 source not in manuscript (likely national aggregate). If Ñuble's transmission pattern differs (e.g., higher occupational silviculture exposure in El Carmen/Pinto with forest-industry economies), the commune-level FSI→case link weakens. Assuming 87% peridomestic in a potentially occupational-dominant region creates confounding by work-related forest exposure unrelated to rodent habitat dynamics
new_findings.fix_hint: Stratify Ñuble cases by exposure setting from SEREMI records (peridomestic vs occupational vs recreational). Report stratum-specific IRR for FSI. If occupational >30%, add forest-worker density covariate or restrict to peridomestic-only subset
new_findings.mcc_rationale: Riquelme 2015 Q1 citation present in prior memory (project_auditoria_Q1_S50_cierre_completo.md), but no empirical verification of 87% in Ñuble study sample found.

new_findings.title: Structural September zero conflated with seasonal factor
new_findings.evidence: 91.2% occurred October–May, with zero cases in September across 23 consecutive years
new_findings.mechanism: Perfect September zero (0/23 years) suggests biological floor (pre-dispersal lag), but season_f 4-level factor treats September identically to other low-risk months. If September is mechanistically different (too early in rodent cycle), pooling it with June-August dilutes seasonal contrast and may bias lag estimates if flowering-to-dispersal window excludes September by design
new_findings.fix_hint: Create 5-level season factor isolating September, or exclude September observations from training as structurally uninformative. Test whether September coefficient differs significantly from other winter months
new_findings.mcc_rationale: Prior evidence confirms 91.2% October–May seasonality pattern and September structural zero, but no explicit sentence integrated in manuscript describing conflation risk.

new_findings.title: FSI compound weights (NDMI+NDVI+NBR2) frozen in Achibueno without Ñuble empirical validation
new_findings.evidence: FSI derived from Landsat 5/7/8 and Sentinel-2 NDMI/NDVI/NBR2 bands via Google Earth Engine...calibrated in Achibueno and frozen before migration to Ñuble (anti-circularity)
new_findings.mechanism: Achibueno (Maule, different bamboo density/species mix per Veblen 1982) may have different spectral signatures for flowering vs drought stress. Freezing weights without Ñuble ground-truth creates measurement error if Ñuble quiloide reflectance differs. Already flagged as S59-B1-03 for undocumented weights, but external-calibration risk is distinct: transferability assumption untested
new_findings.fix_hint: Report correlation between Achibueno-calibrated FSI and field-observed Ñuble flowering events (if available from CONAF records). If correlation <0.6, recalibrate weights using Ñuble-specific training data with proper held-out validation
new_findings.mcc_rationale: Prior evidence documents Achibueno calibration and anti-circularity protocol (project_auditoria_Q1_S50_cierre_completo.md), but lacks Ñuble-specific empirical validation calculation.

new_findings.title: Companion clinical manuscript creates selective outcome reporting risk
new_findings.evidence: A companion clinical analysis of 34 HCPS patients...submitted separately (Contreras et al., Revista Chilena de Infectología)...share no analytical methods, tables, figures, or computed metrics; only publicly available regional descriptors (total case count, case-fatality ratio, ethics approval) are common
new_findings.mechanism: Even with claimed separation, if clinical manuscript reports individual-level covariates (e.g., rural residence, occupation) that correlate with ecological FSI, reviewers/readers cannot assess whether ecological model would hold after individual-level adjustment. Splitting complementary analyses into different journals creates publication bias risk if one manuscript is rejected and findings are not integrated
new_findings.fix_hint: State whether companion manuscript will be cross-referenced if both accepted. Deposit individual-level de-identified covariates (age, sex, rural/urban, occupation category) in Zenodo supplement to enable future ecological fallacy assessment
new_findings.mcc_rationale: Prior evidence (project_estrategia_dual_publication_S42.md) contains explicit dual-publication strategy, Contreras citation, and manuscript separation sentence already integrated.

new_findings.title: Forest cover fraction static covariate not interacted with FSI
new_findings.evidence: static covariates (forest cover fraction, elevation, slope)...zone factor was dropped for redundancy with the comuna random intercept
new_findings.mechanism: FSI signal should be stronger in high-forest-cover communes (more quiloide habitat). No FSI×forest_cover interaction means model assumes FSI slope is identical in 10% vs 90% forested areas. If FSI effect is concentrated in forest-rich communes, pooled estimate is attenuated and precision is overstated
new_findings.fix_hint: Test FSI×forest_cover_fraction interaction via LRT. If p<0.10, stratify by forest tertile and report stratum-specific IRRs. If interaction is significant, add to DAG as effect modifier
new_findings.mcc_rationale: Prior evidence shows static covariate inclusion (GLMM NB2 Final.md) and zone redundancy drop, but no explicit manuscript sentence explaining non-interaction with FSI.

new_findings.title: Case definition switch (IgM pre-2012 vs RT-PCR post-2012) temporal trend not quantified
new_findings.evidence: case definition: IgM seroconversion or RT-PCR positive per MINSAL circular B51/24...already flagged S59-B1-01 for drift
new_findings.mechanism: Pre/post-2012 incidence ratio unadjusted for diagnostics creates temporal confounding with satellite platform transitions
new_findings.fix_hint: Add binary pre/post-2012 covariate to model. Report adjusted IRR and test whether FSI coefficient changes >20% when included. If IRR≠1, conduct sensitivity analysis restricted to 2013-2024 (homogeneous RT-PCR era)
new_findings.mcc_rationale: MINSAL circular B51/24 cited and S59-B1-01 drift flag exists in prior evidence, but no quantified temporal trend calculation (e.g., pre/post-2012 rate ratio) found.

new_findings.title: Log-linear population offset forces proportional incidence assumption
new_findings.evidence: β_pop · log(pop_it)...β_log_pop = +0.614 (p = 0.002)
new_findings.mechanism: Offset with coefficient = 1 forces incidence rate; estimated β_pop = 0.614 ≠ 1.0 suggests sub-linear scaling (larger communes have lower per-capita risk). Model includes log(pop) as covariate rather than offset, which is correct, but labeling as 'offset' in formula is misleading. If reader implements as true offset, forces incorrect proportionality assumption
new_findings.fix_hint: Clarify that log(pop) enters as covariate with estimated slope, not offset. Report LRT comparing free β_pop vs constrained β_pop=1.0 to show offset is rejected. Label as 'population covariate' not 'offset' in formula notation
new_findings.mcc_rationale: Prior evidence includes β_log_pop = +0.614 (p=0.002) from GLMM NB2 Final.md, proportional incidence assumption cited (Bell & Jones 2015), and sentence in manuscript.

new_findings.title: Within-between decomposition attribution without individual-level validation
new_findings.evidence: Within–between decomposition (Bell & Jones 2015) ruled out spatial confounding (ψ_between = −0.018, p = 0.935; Hausman p = 0.291)
new_findings.mechanism: Bell & Jones method separates within-commune temporal variation from between-commune spatial differences, but cannot distinguish whether within-commune FSI effect is causal or confounded by unmeasured time-varying factors (e.g., changing CONAF patrol intensity, rural electrification reducing rodent habitat). Hausman test only detects fixed vs random intercept inconsistency, not unmeasured confounding
new_findings.fix_hint: Acknowledge that within-estimator removes time-invariant confounding but not time-varying confounders. List plausible time-varying confounders (rural development index, CONAF budget) and report whether data exist to test them
new_findings.mcc_rationale: Prior evidence shows Bell & Jones 2015 Q1 citation, within-between decomposition ψ_between=-0.018 p=0.935 and Hausman p=0.291 calculations, and explicit Methods sentence.

new_findings.title: Commune of probable infection vs residence classification without validation
new_findings.evidence: cases reported before that date (n = 103) were originally registered under the SEREMI Biobío regional office and were assigned to their probable commune of infection—rather than residence—following the case reconciliation protocol described in Supplementary Methods
new_findings.mechanism: Retrospective assignment of 103/136 cases (76%) to probable infection commune introduces misclassification bias. If assignment criteria differ systematically by commune characteristics (e.g., rural vs urban, forest cover), spatial risk estimates and FSI associations could be artifactually inflated or dampened. The 87% peridomestic transmission cited elsewhere doesn't validate the spatial assignment accuracy.
new_findings.fix_hint: Report sensitivity analysis comparing results using (1) residence commune, (2) infection commune, (3) cases with documented infection location only. Quantify inter-rater reliability if manual adjudication was used. Report proportion of cases with definitive vs probable spatial assignment.
new_findings.mcc_rationale: Prior evidence documents the SEREMI registration issue and n=103 pre-regionalization cases in multiple locations, but no Q1 citation validates the commune-of-infection assignment protocol nor explicit calculation of misclassification rate.

new_findings.title: FSI compound index weights and validation undocumented
new_findings.evidence: a custom Forest Stress Index (FSI) derived from Landsat 5/7/8 and Sentinel-2 NDMI/NDVI/NBR2 bands via Google Earth Engine...calibrated in Achibueno and frozen before migration to Ñuble
new_findings.mechanism: FSI is the primary exposure but the manuscript provides no formula, band weights, or validation metrics. 'Calibrated in Achibueno' suggests optimization on a different region without documented transferability assessment. Readers cannot reproduce the exposure or assess whether weights capitalize on chance associations in the calibration sample.
new_findings.fix_hint: Report exact FSI formula with band weights. Provide validation metrics (correlation with field-measured Chusquea flowering/rodent density) from Achibueno and Ñuble independently. Report band contribution variance decomposition. Archive GEE script with version-locked band definitions.
new_findings.mcc_rationale: FSI calibration in Achibueno and GEE pipeline documented across multiple sessions (S51, S54, S59), but no Q1 citation for the specific NDMI/NDVI/NBR2 weighting scheme nor validation statistics integrated in manuscript Methods.

new_findings.title: No control for temporal trends in surveillance intensity post-regionalization
new_findings.evidence: Ñuble was administratively established as an independent region by Law 21,033 of 5 September 2017, effective 6 September 2018; cases reported before that date (n = 103) were originally registered under the SEREMI Biobío
new_findings.mechanism: Administrative split creating a new regional SEREMI office typically increases local diagnostic capacity, case investigation resources, and reporting completeness. The 2018 regionalization creates a natural experiment where post-2018 cases may have higher ascertainment than pre-2018 cases under the same ecological conditions, confounding the FSI association with secular surveillance trends.
new_findings.fix_hint: Include a binary covariate (pre/post-2018 regionalization) or spline term in sensitivity analysis. Compare diagnostic test volumes (IgM, RT-PCR) and case investigation completeness metrics before/after split. Test interaction between FSI and period.
new_findings.mcc_rationale: Regionalization date and n=103 pre-2018 cases documented in Estado_Conocimiento and Auditoria_Q1_S50, but no temporal-trend sensitivity analysis nor Q1 citation for surveillance-intensity adjustment methodology.

new_findings.title: Active-burn veto threshold arbitrary without sensitivity analysis
new_findings.evidence: active-burn pixels vetoed (ΔNBR2 < −0.15) to prevent wildfire-scar contamination
new_findings.mechanism: The ΔNBR2 < -0.15 threshold determines which pixels are excluded as wildfire scars. If threshold is too permissive, fire scars contaminate FSI (spurious positive signal); if too stringent, legitimate forest stress is vetoed (attenuated signal). No justification or sensitivity analysis provided for this critical decision boundary.
new_findings.fix_hint: Report sensitivity of FSI-case association (IRR and CI) across ΔNBR2 thresholds {-0.10, -0.15, -0.20, -0.25}. Cite literature justifying -0.15 cutpoint for temperate Nothofagus-Chusquea forests specifically. Report proportion of pixels vetoed at each threshold.
new_findings.mcc_rationale: ΔNBR2 < −0.15 threshold mentioned in Estado_Conocimiento and Sesion_Code_S43, but no Q1 citation justifying the cutoff nor sensitivity analysis (±0.05, ±0.10) reported in manuscript.

new_findings.title: Bell-Jones within-between decomposition applied after lag structure creates NA
new_findings.evidence: All continuous predictors were Bell–Jones within–between decomposed (Bell & Jones 2015), with scaling after NA filtering of the lag structure (anti-leakage)
new_findings.mechanism: Lagging FSI by 5 months creates 105 structural NAs (1.8%) at the start of each commune series. If within-between decomposition is computed on the lag-complete subset only, the 'between' commune means exclude the first 5 months systematically—potentially biasing the between component if early-period values differ (e.g., different forest conditions 2002-2003 vs later periods).
new_findings.fix_hint: Compute commune-specific means for Bell-Jones decomposition using all available data before lagging, then apply lag structure. Report sensitivity comparing (1) means from full series vs (2) means from lag-complete subset. Verify temporal stationarity of FSI within communes.
new_findings.mcc_rationale: Bell & Jones 2015 within-between decomposition cited in Manuscrito_v5_CONDENSED_S60 and Estado_Conocimiento_S50, but no explicit calculation documenting NA-removal order (lag first vs decompose first) nor anti-leakage validation.

new_findings.title: Landsat 5/7/8 and Sentinel-2 sensor harmonization undocumented
new_findings.evidence: a custom Forest Stress Index (FSI) derived from Landsat 5/7/8 and Sentinel-2 NDMI/NDVI/NBR2 bands
new_findings.mechanism: Four satellite platforms with different spectral response functions, radiometric resolution, and temporal coverage are combined into a single index. Without documented cross-calibration, temporal trends in FSI could reflect sensor transitions rather than ecological change (e.g., Landsat 7 SLC failure 2003, Landsat 5 end 2011, Sentinel-2 start 2015).
new_findings.fix_hint: Report sensor-specific contributions by year. Apply harmonized surface reflectance products (e.g., Roy et al. 2016 HLS). Test for discontinuities in FSI time series at known sensor transition dates. Report within-year variance when multiple sensors overlap.
new_findings.mcc_rationale: GEE pipeline documented in GEE_FSI_Reconstruccion_S51 and Estado_Conocimiento_S50, but no Q1 citation for Landsat 5/7/8 ↔ Sentinel-2 harmonization method (e.g., Roy et al. 2016 transform coefficients).

new_findings.title: Ninhue structural zero excluded without contamination analysis
new_findings.evidence: Ninhue registered zero cases (structural zero)
new_findings.mechanism: Ninhue exclusion inflates effect sizes if commune differs systematically; inclusion without structural-zero modeling may dilute signal. No rationale or sensitivity provided.
new_findings.fix_hint: Report Ninhue's forest cover, population, and FSI distribution vs other communes. Conduct sensitivity analysis including Ninhue (1) as observed zero-inflation component, (2) as standard observation, (3) excluded (current). Report how exclusion affects θ and IRR estimates.
new_findings.mcc_rationale: Ninhue structural zero exclusion mentioned in Estado_Conocimiento_S50 and S53_blindaje, but no quantitative contamination analysis (spatial spillover test, residual autocorrelation check) nor manuscript sentence integrated.

new_findings.title: Negative-binomial vs zero-inflated model selection undocumented
new_findings.evidence: The negative-binomial family (Cameron & Trivedi 2005) accommodates rare-event overdispersion in zoonotic surveillance data. ... 97.7% recorded zero cases
new_findings.mechanism: With 97.7% zeros, the data-generating process may include both structural zeros (communes with no Chusquea habitat) and count zeros (random low incidence). Standard negative-binomial assumes all zeros arise from the same process. Zero-inflated negative-binomial or hurdle models could better distinguish these mechanisms, potentially improving calibration and discrimination.
new_findings.fix_hint: Report Vuong test comparing NB2 vs ZINB. Present zero-inflation parameter estimates for communes. Test whether structural zeros correlate with forest cover or elevation. Report AIC/BIC comparison and rootogram to assess zero-count fit.
new_findings.mcc_rationale: Cameron & Trivedi 2005 cited for NB2 family in GLMM_NB2_Final.md, 97.7% zero inflation documented, but no AIC/BIC table comparing NB2 vs zero-inflated alternatives (ZINB, hurdle) integrated in manuscript.

new_findings.title: Commune spatial scale arbitrary relative to rodent home range
new_findings.evidence: comuna–month panel in Ñuble Region, south-central Chile (21 comunas, population ≈487,866)
new_findings.mechanism: Oligoryzomys longicaudatus home range is ~0.5-2 hectares (Silva-Rodríguez et al. 2008), while communes span 100-2000+ km². Aggregating cases and FSI to administrative units 6-7 orders of magnitude larger than the biological process scale introduces ecological fallacy and may dilute fine-scale exposure-outcome associations. Optimal spatial grain is undetermined.
new_findings.fix_hint: Report sensitivity using alternative spatial grains: (1) 1×1 km grid cells, (2) 5×5 km grid, (3) commune. Compare IRR effect sizes and CIs across scales. Discuss Modified Areal Unit Problem (MAUP). Report intra-commune variance in FSI to quantify aggregation-induced information loss.
new_findings.mcc_rationale: Commune scale (21 comunas) documented in Estado_Conocimiento_S50 and Manuscrito_v5, but no Q1 citation justifying spatial resolution relative to O. longicaudatus home-range (~0.5–2 ha) nor sensitivity analysis at finer scales.

new_findings.title: Socioeconomic and occupational confounders uncontrolled
new_findings.evidence: β_log_pop = +0.614 (p = 0.002) ... Andes-virus hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome is classified among neglected tropical diseases linked to poverty and ecosystem disturbance
new_findings.mechanism: Population size proxies both denominator (exposure opportunity) and urbanization/poverty. Commune-level poverty, agricultural employment, and housing quality are established HCPS risk factors (Prist et al. 2021; Abramson et al. 2003) that correlate with both forest proximity (FSI exposure) and case detection, creating confounding bias. The population coefficient may reflect unmeasured socioeconomic factors rather than pure exposure opportunity.
new_findings.fix_hint: Include commune-level covariates: Social Priority Index, % agricultural employment, median household income, % rural housing. Test for residual confounding using E-values for SES-case association. Conduct stratified analysis by urban vs rural communes.
new_findings.mcc_rationale: Population effect β=+0.614 (p=0.002) documented in GLMM_NB2_Final.md and poverty linkage mentioned, but no Q1 citation for socioeconomic/occupational confounder control nor explicit adjustment calculation in manuscript.

new_findings.title: Seasonality modeled as fixed factor without cyclic constraint
new_findings.evidence: β_season · season_f_t ... season_f a 4-level categorical factor
new_findings.mechanism: Four-level categorical seasonality imposes discontinuities at quarter boundaries (Dec→Jan, Mar→Apr, etc.) and uses 3 degrees of freedom without enforcing temporal smoothness. Cyclic splines or Fourier terms would more realistically capture gradual seasonal transitions and reduce parameter burden, potentially improving out-of-sample prediction.
new_findings.fix_hint: Compare current 4-level factor against (1) cyclic cubic spline with 4 df, (2) Fourier terms (2 harmonics), using out-of-sample log score. Report AIC and residual temporal autocorrelation. Visualize fitted seasonal curves to assess boundary artifacts.
new_findings.mcc_rationale: All prior snippets are empty strings. No Q1 citation, calculation, or manuscript sentence found regarding cyclic/non-cyclic seasonality factor constraint.

new_findings.title: Companion study overlap with undisclosed shared authorship
new_findings.evidence: A companion clinical analysis of 34 HCPS patients treated at Hospital Clínico Herminda Martín, Chillán, 2010–2025 has been submitted separately (Contreras et al., Revista Chilena de Infectología)
new_findings.mechanism: Companion manuscripts with overlapping data sources, study periods, and single-author bylines risk fragmenting findings for publication maximization (salami slicing). While ICMJE criteria are cited, absence of co-author disclosure prevents assessment of intellectual independence. Reviewers cannot verify that case-fatality and case-count data are presented optimally in each venue.
new_findings.fix_hint: Disclose all authors on companion manuscript. Provide table mapping which data elements (cases, CFR, time periods) appear in each manuscript. Confirm both journals received pre-submission notification per ICMJE guidelines. Archive both manuscripts together at Zenodo to document complementarity.
new_findings.mcc_rationale: Prior evidence confirms dual-manuscript strategy (project_dual_manuscript_factory_S44 multiple hits), companion clinical submission explicitly documented, and manuscript text integrated in Paper_EID_Final.md.

new_objections.title: Case assignment to 'probable commune of infection' is outcome-dependent spatial imputation masquerading as data collection
new_objections.critique: Methods §5.5.1 states pre-2018 cases (n=103, 76% of dataset) were 'assigned to their probable commune of infection—rather than residence—following the case reconciliation protocol described in Supplementary Methods.' This is outcome-dependent spatial re-allocation after the fact. You've created a circular training loop: use cases to infer infection location, then train a spatial model on those inferred locations.
new_objections.suggested_response: Sensitivity analysis restricting to post-2018 residence-based cases (n=33) or treating pre-2018 as region-level aggregates only. If core results collapse with n=33, the entire spatial structure is an artifact of retrospective imputation. Publish the imputation algorithm in main text, not supplements.

new_objections.title: E-value 2.07 invoked without sensitivity analysis or requisite confounding DAG
new_objections.critique: Figure 2 caption annotates 'E-value = 2.07 for the FSI lag-5 coefficient' yet no DAG with unmeasured confounders appears, no prior literature quantifying unmeasured confounder strength is cited, and no sensitivity analysis varies E-value assumptions. E-value is not a talisman. VanderWeele (2019): 'E-value alone is insufficient without substantive knowledge of plausible confounders and their likely magnitudes.'
new_objections.suggested_response: Remove E-value annotation or provide: (1) DAG with ≥2 candidate unmeasured confounders (e.g., microhabitat humidity, rodent serology at t-5), (2) literature-derived bounds on U→FSI and U→HCPS associations, (3) bias formulas under VanderWeele framework showing IRR remains <1.0 under plausible U scenarios.

new_objections.title: Burn-in exclusion (folds 2011–2014, n=33 events) is post-hoc model rescue contradicting pre-specification claims
new_objections.critique: Methods §5.5.3: 'first four folds θ exceeded 1×10⁶...we pre-specified these as burn-in (Addendum v1.1, lock date 2026-04-04).' But Addendum v1.1 was written AFTER observing θ instability—classic post-hoc rationalization. Bergmeir/Cerqueira cite time-series CV literature; your model is spatial panel. Excluding 24% of events (33/136) based on nuisance-parameter behavior is outcome-driven model pruning.
new_objections.suggested_response: Report 14-fold results as primary or disclose that burn-in decision was post-hoc conditional on θ >10⁶ in early folds. If 10-fold CI width inflates >2× vs 14-fold, state so. Alternatively, use Poisson likelihood for burn-in period as pre-specified fallback. Current framing falsely implies априори design.

new_objections.title: Decision curve analysis threshold range [0.05,0.20] remains operationally arbitrary and unexplained
new_objections.critique: Methods §5.5.8 and Results §5.6.6 annotate p_t ∈ [0.05,0.20] as 'operational range' with zero clinical/operational justification. What cost ratio does p_t=0.05 encode? Why not [0.03,0.15] or [0.10,0.25]? Vickers et al. (2019): DCA threshold should map to harm:benefit ratio. You provide none. Annotating an arbitrary interval as 'operational' is window-dressing.
new_objections.suggested_response: Justify threshold range via explicit harm:benefit elicitation (e.g., cost of unnecessary CONAF patrol vs missed outbreak) or remove 'operational' label and present full DCA curve [0,0.30]. If no stakeholder elicitation occurred, state this and label annotated range as 'illustrative' only.

new_objections.title: Calibration is entirely absent despite 'probabilistic forecast' framing throughout manuscript
new_objections.critique: Title, abstract, and Discussion tout 'three-tier probabilistic early warning system' yet zero calibration metrics appear. No calibration plot, no Hosmer-Lemeshow, no calibration slope/intercept. Log score conflates calibration and discrimination; you cannot infer well-calibrated probabilities from log score alone (Van Calster et al. 2016). If p(Tier 1)=0.08 but observed rate is 0.02, your probabilities mislead despite decent log score.
new_objections.suggested_response: Add calibration plot (predicted vs observed rates in deciles) and calibration slope ± SE for Tier 1 and Tier 2. If calibration is poor (slope ≠1, intercept ≠0), recalibrate via Platt scaling or report uncalibrated status. Current omission contradicts probabilistic forecasting standards (Gneiting & Raftery 2007 §5).

new_objections.title: Retrospective case assignment to 'probable commune of infection' creates circular validation
new_objections.critique: Manuscript states cases were 'assigned to their probable commune of infection—rather than residence—following the case reconciliation protocol described in Supplementary Methods' (Methods §5.5.1). This is outcome-dependent spatial imputation: the dependent variable (cases_it by commune) is itself constructed using information that post-dates exposure. Walk-forward validation is invalidated when the outcome being predicted was assembled retrospectively using auxiliary data unavailable at forecast time.
new_objections.suggested_response: Provide complete protocol in main text showing case assignment relied solely on prospectively recorded exposure history (residence during incubation period) or restrict analysis to residence-based assignment and acknowledge reduced spatial precision as a limitation. Current approach conflates prediction with retrospective reconstruction.

new_objections.title: Burn-in exclusion (folds 2011–2014) is post-hoc outcome-dependent model rescue
new_objections.critique: Authors exclude first four folds citing 'θ exceeded 1×10⁶' and cite time-series CV literature (Bergmeir, Cerqueira) for 'burn-in' justification. But: (1) negative-binomial θ→∞ is Poisson convergence under sparse data—the model is telling you there is no overdispersion to estimate, not that it needs 'burn-in'; (2) excluding folds because dispersion parameter is unstable is selecting on the dependent variable; (3) pre-specification addendum v1.1 filed same day as first script execution undermines temporal firewall.
new_objections.suggested_response: Report θ and all metrics for full 14 folds as primary analysis. If sparse-data instability invalidates inference, acknowledge inadequate sample size for confirmatory claims. Time-series burn-in does not license excluding folds where the model converges to a valid Poisson limit. Current approach is post-hoc model rescue disguised as methodological rigor.

new_objections.title: E-value 2.07 cited without requisite confounding structure or sensitivity analysis
new_objections.critique: E-value 2.07 for FSI lag-5 IRR appears in Figure 2 legend and Discussion but: (1) no DAG specifies hypothesized unmeasured confounders; (2) no sensitivity analysis explores plausible magnitudes; (3) E-value 2.07 implies unmeasured confounder must have RR≥2.07 with both exposure and outcome—far from protective. In observational ecological design with retrospective spatial imputation, this threshold is easily exceeded by unmodeled rodent migration, land-use change, or healthcare access gradients.
new_objections.suggested_response: Remove E-value or provide formal sensitivity analysis with DAG specifying candidate confounders (rodent abundance, harvest patterns, healthcare access), their plausible effect sizes, and whether E-value 2.07 is realistically protective. Current invocation is ritualistic citation without inferential weight.

new_objections.title: Decision curve threshold range [0.05, 0.20] remains operationally unjustified
new_objections.critique: DCA operational range p_t∈[0.05,0.20] is annotated in Methods §5.5.8 and Results §5.6.6 as 'operational range' but no clinical, resource, or policy rationale is provided. What decision does p_t=0.05 vs 0.20 correspond to? What is the harm:benefit ratio embedded in these thresholds? DCA without explicit decision context is probabilistic theater. Authors state 'Tier-1 net benefit exceeded Bortman by ≈3× at p_t=0.05' but do not justify why 5% risk threshold merits action.
new_objections.suggested_response: Justify threshold range by mapping p_t values to operational decisions (e.g., 0.05=CONAF patrol activation cost-benefit, 0.20=mass education trigger). If no such mapping exists, state DCA is exploratory sensitivity analysis across arbitrary thresholds and remove 'operational' framing. Current presentation implies decision-theoretic rigor without providing decision structure.

new_objections.title: Calibration entirely absent despite 'probabilistic forecast' claims throughout
new_objections.critique: Manuscript frames output as 'probabilistic early warning' (Title, Abstract, Discussion) and invokes 'strictly proper scoring rules' (Methods §5.5.5) but provides zero calibration assessment: no calibration plot, no reliability diagram, no goodness-of-fit test. Log score rewards sharpness and penalizes miscalibration but does not decompose them. Model may achieve acceptable log score while being systematically overconfident or underconfident—critical failure mode for operational EWS.
new_objections.suggested_response: Add calibration belt (Nattino et al. 2016) or binned calibration plot with confidence bands for Tier 1 and Tier 2 predictions. If model is miscalibrated, acknowledge this explicitly and discuss implications for operational deployment. Proper scoring rules are necessary but insufficient for probabilistic forecast validation.

new_objections.title: Case assignment protocol circular with outcome—'probable commune of infection' explicitly depends on case status
new_objections.critique: Methods §5.5.1 states 'cases…were assigned to their probable commune of infection…following the case reconciliation protocol described in Supplementary Methods.' This is outcome-dependent spatial imputation: you cannot assign a case to a location *because* it is a case, then validate a model predicting cases at that location. The validation set is contaminated by the assignment rule.
new_objections.suggested_response: Report separate analyses: (1) residence-based (no imputation), (2) infection-site if prospectively recorded, (3) sensitivity analysis showing BSS under random spatial misclassification ±1 adjacent commune. If 'probable infection site' was inferred post hoc from model covariates (e.g., high FSI communes), the entire validation is circular and unpublishable.

new_objections.title: Burn-in exclusion is post-hoc rescue disguised as pre-specification—Addendum v1.1 lock date is *after* seeing fold performance
new_objections.critique: Methods §5.5.3 cites 'pre-specified burn-in (Addendum v1.1, lock date 2026-04-04)' but §5.5.9 states the Addendum commit is 40 minutes before first computation script. If you observed θ→∞ in folds 2011–2014, then wrote the Addendum, that is post-hoc outcome-dependent exclusion. Time-series CV literature (Bergmeir 2018) does not license excluding folds after seeing results.
new_objections.suggested_response: Provide Addendum v1.1 submission timestamp to *external* registry (e.g., OSF) with blinded hash of fold assignments, provably before running scripts. If unavailable, report 14-fold results as primary, 10-fold as sensitivity, and explicitly label burn-in exclusion as post-hoc data-dependent—or withdraw the pre-specification claim entirely.

new_objections.title: Calibration assessment entirely absent despite 'probabilistic forecast' framing—log score does not substitute for calibration plots
new_objections.critique: Results §5.6.3–5.6.6 report log score, BSS, PR-AUC, decision curves—but zero calibration assessment (observed vs predicted probability plots, calibration slope, intercept, Hosmer-Lemeshow). Log score penalizes miscalibration but does not visualize it. For operational EWS deployment, calibration is non-negotiable (Steyerberg 2019; Van Calster 2016).
new_objections.suggested_response: Add calibration plots (decile or flexible calibration curves with loess) for Tier 1 and Tier 2, stratified by fold if sample permits. Report calibration slope and intercept with 95% CI. If severe miscalibration exists (slope ≠1, intercept ≠0), apply Platt scaling or isotonic regression recalibration before operational deployment claims.

new_objections.title: E-value 2.07 for unmeasured confounding is weak and invoked without requisite DAG or sensitivity analysis structure
new_objections.critique: Figure 2 annotates 'E-value = 2.07' for FSI lag-5 IRR. E-values quantify the minimum strength of unmeasured confounding to explain away an association (VanderWeele & Ding 2017). 2.07 is modest—easily reached by uncontrolled socioeconomic, housing, or rodent-abundance variables. No DAG, no measured/unmeasured confounder list, no Monte Carlo sensitivity analysis (Ding & VanderWeele 2016).
new_objections.suggested_response: Present a causal DAG enumerating measured covariates (temperature, seasonality, population) and plausible unmeasured confounders (rodent trapping indices, household construction quality, rurality index). Conduct Rosenbaum-style sensitivity analysis or Bayesian bias analysis (Lash et al. 2021) under realistic confounding scenarios. If E-value is sole sensitivity metric, state explicitly that causal inference is speculative.

new_objections.title: Decision curve threshold range [0.05, 0.20] lacks operational anchor—no cost-benefit specification or stakeholder elicitation
new_objections.critique: Methods §5.5.8 and Results §5.6.6 report DCA over p_t ∈ [0.05, 0.20] as 'operational range' but provide zero justification. What is the cost of a false alarm (CONAF patrol, SEREMI education campaign)? What is the value of averting one HCPS case (CFR 28%, ICU cost)? Without cost parameters or stakeholder-elicited thresholds, the range is arbitrary (Vickers 2006 original paper).
new_objections.suggested_response: Elicit cost-benefit parameters from MINSAL, SEREMI, CONAF: (a) cost per false-positive alert, (b) value of statistical life (VSL) or disability-adjusted life year (DALY) averted, (c) acceptable false-alarm rate. Derive threshold p_t from harm:benefit ratio. If unavailable, label DCA threshold range as illustrative and defer operational deployment until cost-effectiveness analysis is complete.

new_objections.title: Case assignment to 'probable commune of infection' remains outcome-dependent and creates fatal circularity in walk-forward validation
new_objections.critique: Methods §5.5.1 states 103 pre-2018 cases 'assigned to probable commune of infection following the case reconciliation protocol in Supplementary Methods'—but the manuscript provides NO protocol description. This is outcome-dependent spatial imputation: case status determines spatial assignment, then spatial predictors are trained on those assignments, yielding circular validation. The 'Supplementary Methods' deflection is unacceptable; reviewers cannot audit what is not present.
new_objections.suggested_response: Describe the complete case assignment algorithm in main text Methods (≤100 words). If assignment used clinical interview residence vs exposure history, state explicitly. If algorithmic imputation was used, describe features and validation. If protocol is unavailable, acknowledge spatial assignment as a study limitation and conduct sensitivity analysis excluding pre-2018 cases (n=103 of 136, 76% of dataset).

new_objections.title: Burn-in exclusion lock date (2026-04-04) post-dates first walk-forward output by physical impossibility—prespecification claim is false
new_objections.critique: Methods §5.5.3 states 'Addendum v1.1, lock date 2026-04-04' prespecified burn-in, and §5.5.9 claims git timestamp '33–40 minutes before first output.' But 2026 is in the future. If intended as 2025-04-04, reviewers need filesystem audit trail proving the 4-fold exclusion rule existed before seeing fold 1–4 θ estimates. Current framing implies post-hoc rescue disguised as prespecification.
new_objections.suggested_response: Correct calendar year typo. Provide complete git log excerpt showing Addendum v1.1 commit timestamp, first θ diagnostic output timestamp, and first burn-in-exclusion script timestamp. If Addendum was written after seeing θ>1e6, acknowledge post-hoc decision and reframe as sensitivity analysis rather than prespecified protocol, reporting both 10-fold and 14-fold as co-primary.

new_objections.title: Calibration assessment entirely absent despite 'probabilistic forecast' framing—log score does not substitute for calibration plots
new_objections.critique: Title/Abstract/Discussion claim 'probabilistic early warning system' and 'forecast skill,' yet Results contain zero calibration assessment (observed vs predicted frequency, calibration slope, intercept, or Hosmer-Lemeshow). Log score penalizes miscalibration but does not quantify it. A model can achieve good log score while systematically under- or over-predicting rare-event probabilities (Gneiting 2011).
new_objections.suggested_response: Add calibration plot (deciles of predicted probability vs observed frequency) for Tier 1 and Tier 2 in main text Figure panel. Report calibration slope and intercept from logistic recalibration model (Van Calster et al. 2016). If systematic over/underprediction exists, discuss implications for operational threshold selection in DCA. If sample size precludes stable decile estimates, acknowledge as limitation and report grouped calibration (3–5 bins).

new_objections.title: E-value 2.07 for unmeasured confounding is weak, presented without DAG or quantitative bias analysis, and cannot support causal inference claims
new_objections.critique: Figure 2 caption states 'E-value = 2.07 for FSI lag-5 coefficient.' VanderWeele & Ding (2017) define E-value as minimum unmeasured confounder strength to explain away an observed association. E=2.07 is routinely exceeded by measured confounders in observational ecology (socioeconomic, land-use, surveillance intensity). No DAG, no sensitivity analysis, no quantification of plausible confounding magnitudes.
new_objections.suggested_response: Remove E-value from Figure 2 or reframe explicitly: 'E-value 2.07 indicates the observed IRR could be explained by unmeasured confounding of moderate strength; causal interpretation requires external validation.' If retained, provide DAG in Supplement identifying candidate unmeasured confounders (surveillance effort, land-use change, rodent control programs) and justify why each is implausible to reach RR≥2.07 for both confounder–exposure and confounder–outcome.

new_objections.title: Decision curve threshold range [0.05, 0.20] remains operationally unjustified—no cost–benefit ratio elicitation or stakeholder input documented
new_objections.critique: Methods §5.5.8 and Results §5.6.6 report DCA 'operational range p_t ∈ [0.05, 0.20] annotated' but provide zero justification. Vickers & Elkin (2006) require threshold selection to reflect harm:benefit ratio; p_t=0.05 implies treating 20 false-positives per true-positive is acceptable. No cost of false alarm (CONAF patrol deployment, community anxiety) or benefit of true detection (life-years saved, hospitalizations averted) is quantified.
new_objections.suggested_response: Either (1) report stakeholder workshop or expert elicitation deriving the 5–20% range from operational constraints (e.g., CONAF has capacity for ≤10 patrol deployments per season, implying p_t≈0.10 given base rate 0.023), or (2) remove 'operational range' language and present DCA across full [0, 0.25] continuum with annotation: 'Threshold selection requires jurisdiction-specific cost–benefit elicitation beyond this study's scope.'

new_objections.title: Case assignment protocol remains outcome-dependent despite 'Supplementary Methods' deflection
new_objections.critique: Text states 'assigned to their probable commune of infection...following the case reconciliation protocol described in Supplementary Methods' — this is redirection, not resolution. Case location imputation that depends on case status creates circular validation when those locations become the spatial unit of outcome measurement. The walk-forward train/test split is contaminated at the root.
new_objections.suggested_response: Conduct sensitivity analysis restricting to residence-only cases (n=33 post-2018) or acknowledge the validation is internally circular and restrict claims to hypothesis-generation pending prospective deployment with pre-specified case ascertainment that does not use outcome data.

new_objections.title: Burn-in lock date 2026-04-04 remains physically impossible for test-fold output already generated
new_objections.critique: Addendum v1.1 lock timestamp (2026-04-04) cannot predate walk-forward outputs from folds 2011–2014 when those folds were computed in 2023–2024 calendar time. The manuscript claims 'git timestamps confirm addendum precedes script by 40 minutes' but does not address the physical impossibility of locking *after* seeing fold performance. This is post-hoc rescue.
new_objections.suggested_response: Acknowledge the burn-in exclusion was data-driven after observing θ→Poisson behavior in early folds. Report both 10-fold and 14-fold metrics as co-primary (not 'sensitivity') and remove prespecification language around burn-in. The decision is scientifically defensible but not pre-registered.

new_objections.title: Calibration assessment entirely absent despite repeated 'probabilistic forecast' framing
new_objections.critique: Text claims 'logarithmic score...stable in rare-event regimes' and 'probabilistic EWS' throughout, yet provides zero calibration assessment: no calibration plots, no Hosmer-Lemeshow, no reliability diagrams. Log score rewards sharpness but does not guarantee calibration. BSS vs Bortman baseline (68%) measures discrimination, not probability alignment.
new_objections.suggested_response: Add calibration belt plots (Nattino et al. 2016) or bin-level observed-vs-expected plots for each tier, stratified by walk-forward fold. If calibration is poor, acknowledge that forecasts are discriminative rankings only and remove 'probabilistic' framing or qualify it as 'uncalibrated probabilistic scores.'

new_objections.title: E-value 2.07 cited without requisite confounding structure or quantitative bias analysis
new_objections.critique: Figure 2 annotates 'E-value = 2.07 for FSI lag-5 coefficient' but provides no DAG, no discussion of which unmeasured confounders could plausibly reach RR=2.07, and no sensitivity analysis around the observational IRR=0.734. E-values are screening tools, not shields; invoking one without structure is ritual compliance, not causal inference.
new_objections.suggested_response: Either (1) provide DAG with candidate unmeasured confounders (rodent serology, wildfire history, human mobility) and argue why RR≥2.07 is implausible for each, or (2) remove E-value annotation and present FSI coefficient as associational predictor only, deferring causal claims to future experimental or instrumental-variable designs.

new_objections.title: Decision curve threshold range [0.05, 0.20] remains operationally unjustified and arbitrary
new_objections.critique: Text states 'operational range p_t ∈ [0.05, 0.20] annotated' but provides zero cost-benefit elicitation, stakeholder input, or resource-constraint modeling to anchor these thresholds. DCA is meaningless without a priori cost ratios; annotating a range post-hoc is curve-shopping. The range conveniently excludes p_t>0.20 where net benefit may collapse.
new_objections.suggested_response: Document stakeholder elicitation with MINSAL/CONAF on cost of false alarm (patrol deployment, public messaging) vs cost of missed outbreak, translate to harm:benefit ratio, and solve for implied threshold. If unavailable, state 'we explored p_t ∈ [0.05,0.20] for illustrative purposes; operational thresholds require prospective stakeholder engagement.'

new_candidates.title: A Calibrated Multi-Task Ensemble Architecture for Biomedical Risk Prediction
new_candidates.abstract: Risk stratification of impaired glycemic control remains a major challenge in biomedical data analysis due to heterogeneous metabolic, behavioral, and therapeutic factors observed in large-scale populations. This study proposes a calibrated and interpretable decision–support framework, termed Calibrated Multi-Task Stacking Ensemble (CMSE), for joint modeling of clinically related glycemic outcomes. The framework integrates demographic variables, lipid profiles, renal and inflammatory biomarkers, dietary and smoking indicators, and therapy-related features within a unified predictive architecture. Robust modeling is ensured through leakage-aware preprocessing, quantile-based Winsorization, out-of-fold stacking, and isotonic calibration of probabilistic outputs. The physiological coherence b
new_candidates.reason: Calibrated ensemble with isotonic calibration for risk prediction; supports our Brier/RPS scoring methodology

new_candidates.title: Imputation Free Deep Survival Prediction with Conditional Variational Autoencoders
new_candidates.abstract: Abstract Electronic Health Records (EHRs) provide rich opportunities for developing risk prediction tools to support clinical decision-making, yet they are inherently incomplete because data are recorded selectively during routine care. Such missingness may be informative, reflecting clinical judgment and patient status, and missing data patterns can shift between model development and real-world deployment. These challenges limit the reliability and transportability of predictive models in healthcare settings. We propose an imputation-free framework that jointly trains Conditional Variational Autoencoders with deep survival models to enable risk prediction directly from incomplete EHR data. We demonstrate the approach using the deep survival model DeSurv and evaluate its performance throu
new_candidates.reason: Imputation-free survival modeling with incomplete observational data; addresses missing covariate handling

new_candidates.title: Statistical post‐processing of operational dual‐resolution wind‐speed ensemble forecasts
new_candidates.abstract: Abstract Weather forecasting presents several challenges, including the chaotic nature of the atmosphere and the high computational demands of numerical weather prediction models. To achieve the most accurate predictions, the ideal scenario involves the lowest possible horizontal resolution and the largest ensemble size. This study provides a detailed comparative analysis of the forecast skill of the raw and post‐processed medium‐ and extended‐range wind‐speed ensemble forecasts of the European Centre for Medium‐Range Weather Forecasts issued at 9 km and 36 km horizontal resolutions respectively, and their various mixtures. We utilize the ensemble model output statistic approach for forecast calibration with three different spatial training data selection techniques. First, we investigate
new_candidates.reason: EMOS post-processing and ensemble calibration methods directly applicable to our FSI-based probabilistic forecasting

new_candidates.title: Playing the Odds: Agentic LLMs for Real-Time NBA Forecasting and Market Betting
new_candidates.abstract: Predicting the outcomes of professional basketball games is a challenging problem due to the intrinsic stochastic uncertainty of sports competitions and the heterogeneity of relevant information sources. While existing approaches in sports analytics primarily rely on structured historical statistics, such methods often struggle to incorporate timely and unstructured information. In this paper, we propose a unified framework that leverages large language models for probabilistic forecasting and decision making for NBA games and their prediction markets. Our approach combines specialized information retrieval agents with multiple role-based LLM predictors, whose forecasts are aggregated into the final forecasting probabilities. These probabilities are then operationalized through a fractiona
new_candidates.reason: Probabilistic forecasting with aggregated predictions and operational decision-making; parallels our early warning tiers

new_candidates.title: Probabilistic Forecasting for Day-ahead Electricity Prices, Battery Trading Strategies and the Economic Evaluation of Predictive Accuracy
new_candidates.abstract: Electricity price forecasting supports decision-making in energy markets and asset operation. Probabilistic forecasts are increasingly adopted to explicitly quantify uncertainty, typically issued as quantile predictions or ensembles of the full predictive distribution. However, how improvements in statistical forecast quality translate into economic value remains unclear. Battery storage arbitrage in day-ahead markets is a popular application-based benchmark for this purpose. We analyze quantile-based trading strategies (QBTS) and identify two critical flaws: they do not incentivize honest probabilistic forecasting and they ignore the intertemporal dependence structure of electricity prices. We therefore frame battery optimization as a stochastic program based on fully probabilistic foreca
new_candidates.reason: Probabilistic forecasting with scoring rules (similar to Brier/RPS) and operational decision-making framework

new_candidates.title: Evaluating the quality of the quantified uncertainty for (Re)calibration of data-driven regression models
new_candidates.abstract: • First systematic benchmark of calibration metrics in uncertainty quantification • Calibration metrics frequently produce inconsistent and conflicting evaluations • Using different calibration metrics in regression allows cherry-picking of results • The ENCE and CWC emerge as most reliable metrics for miscalibration detection • Metrics need ≥ 500-1000 samples for robust evaluation in recalibration studies In safety-critical applications data-driven models must not only be accurate but also provide reliable uncertainty estimates. This property, commonly referred to as calibration, is essential for risk-aware decision-making. In regression a wide variety of calibration metrics and recalibration methods have emerged. However, these metrics differ significantly in their definitions, assumptio
new_candidates.reason: Systematic benchmark of calibration metrics (ENCE/CWC) highly relevant to our Brier/BSS/RPS evaluation

new_candidates.title: Nematodes inhabiting the gastrointestinal tract and accessory organs of cricetid rodents in Argentina and Chile: a systematic review
new_candidates.abstract: South American ecosystems harbour diverse cricetid rodents and their associated nematode parasites, with implications for ecology and zoonotic risk.We conducted a systematic review following the PRISMA guidelines to catalogue nematode taxa reported from the digestive tract and accessory organs of cricetid rodents in Argentina and Chile.Searches were carried out in five databases (PubMed, WoS, Scopus, SciELO, and Google Scholar) up to January 10, 2026.Parasitological descriptors, such as Prevalence (P), mean abundance ( M A), and mean intensity ( M I) were estimated.We analysed 53 studies examining 4400 rodents (33 species), with Abrothrix olivacea (n=1000 specimens) and Akodon azarae (n=775 specimens) most frequently sampled.Ninety-two nematode taxa were reported, with A. olivacea exhibiti
new_candidates.reason: Systematic cricetid rodent ecology in Argentina/Chile, relevant to reservoir host context
